column2: Statement

Desktop mail clients showing messages stuck in Outbox while webmail works — caused by tenant security policy / SMTP AUTH being disabled (Security Defaults / Conditional Access) and resolved by selective re‑enable or conditional‑access adjustment / hybrid connector fixes. Example: an Exchange/Outbox incident where investigators found SMTP AUTH blocked by tenant security defaults and remediated by targeted policy/connector changes [This is recorded in the Exchange / SMTP auth incident notes]

- External partners shown as “Offline” / delayed chat and some calls not ringing — documented causes include tenant federation/presence configuration and Direct‑Routing/SBC provisioning or NAT/session problems; fixes involved applying the Teams Federation/Direct‑Routing blueprint and correcting SBC/provisioning rules (temporary translation rules proved fixes in prior cases)

- Large (20–30 page) MFP scan reported “sent” but not delivered — prior cases show SMTP relay size limits or relay ACLs rejecting large messages; the logged SMTP relay rejection “MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT” was the explicit diagnostic line leading to mailbox/delivery fixes and/or relay config changes

- Printer jobs vanish from queue at site (Basel) — documented precedents include network/VLAN segmentation or print‑share publishing failures and also Windows Update + VM‑restore sequences that left print queues paused or shares unpublished; recorded remediations included restarting spooler, re‑publishing the network share (disable/re‑enable), correcting VLAN/routing rules or performing infra/VM forensic follow‑up when restores caused BSODs

- Multi‑domain, simultaneous outages of the sort you describe historically required escalation to engineering / second_level_support (Network & Security, Identity/Platform, Mail Team, Infrastructure). Several prior multi‑system incidents (portal/WAF, ADFS/regression, printing + VM restore) were handled at second level in the indexed corpus

Action (documented): Classify/route this ticket to second_level_support (engage Identity/Entra, Mail/Exchange, Network & Security, Print/Infra). Prior, identical multi‑domain outages were escalated to specialist engineers rather than resolved at 1st‑line
Evidence: multi‑domain patterns in indexed incidents required engineering (see Exchange/SMTP AUTH, Teams federation/Direct Routing, SAP printing).

Step 2.1 (documented): Verify whether SMTP AUTH has been disabled by tenant Security Defaults / Conditional Access and whether the affected users are subject to that policy. Prior case showed desktop Outlook sending failures while OWA worked because SMTP AUTH was disabled by a security setting

Evidence to collect: Azure AD Conditional Access change or Security Defaults enablement, Exchange/Hybrid connector logs showing 535 / “authentication unsuccessful” events, and per‑user sign‑in events showing CA denies

Step 2.2 (documented remedial options used before): If tenant policy blocked legacy SMTP AUTH and these users require legacy SMTP, apply the documented mitigation used in the prior case: either (A) create a scoped/conditional exception to allow SMTP AUTH for the specific users or devices, or (B) update the mail client configuration to use modern authenticated submission (or use OWA) while a scoped remediation is applied. The historical ticket shows targeted policy changes and selective connector adjustments were used to restore desktop send for affected users
Mark as: documented remediation (see TI‑1548823 example)

Step 2.3 (documented): Check Exchange logs for EventID/SMTP 535 lines and the hybrid/send connector logs to confirm whether the desktop SMTP submission attempts are being blocked by CA or by the connector. Prior tickets used these log lines to prove the root cause.

Step 3.1 (documented): Confirm whether a tenant‑level federation/presence or external access policy changed in the same change window. Prior incidents used the Teams/Federation blueprint and tenant admin checks to identify presence/federation policy changes that caused external partners to show offline
Evidence to collect: Teams Admin Center audit entries for federation/presence policy, tenant federation settings, and any recent provisioning changes that altered presence behavior

Step 3.2 (documented): Check SBC/Direct‑Routing provisioning and translation rules for recently applied changes or missing number‑ranges; prior Direct‑Routing incidents reproduced the failure, applied a temporary translation rule (which immediately fixed calls), then applied the permanent correction
Evidence to collect: SBC logs, carrier traces, and translation table entries; run test calls while capturing SIP traces to reproduce failure

Step 3.3 (RECOMMENDED): If presence is impacted but federation policies look unchanged, request Microsoft/tenant‑side sign‑in logs for affected external partner UPNs to confirm whether presence/availability tokens are being delivered — records show sign‑in/token logs were used in label/presence investigations

Step 4.1 (documented): Check the SMTP relay logs for explicit rejection reason lines (previous tickets captured “MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT” as the decisive evidence) and collect device MFP logs showing the SMTP dialogue timestamps

Step 4.2 (documented): If relay rejects due to size, implement the prior documented remediation: adjust the relay max‑message size or adjust MFP scan settings (reduce DPI/enable PDF compression) so the MFP messages fit within relay limits. Prior incidents fixed large‑scan failures by adjusting relay policy or scan profile

Step 5.1 (documented): Check the print server spooler and queue state; prior cases recorded “Paused” queues and solved by restarting the print spooler and re‑publishing the network share (disable/re‑enable publish) to clear stuck SAP jobs

Step 5.2 (documented): Check whether a recent network/VLAN change or firewall rule separated the printer VLAN from the print server (previous Basel incidents showed a new Voice/Device VLAN or NAT/session timeout caused device reachability and printing failures)

Step 5.3 (documented remediation options used in prior cases): Restart spooler, re‑publish share (disable then enable), and if the print server was recently restored from VM snapshot, collect hypervisor restore logs and examine for post‑restore BSODs (prior SAP printing incident used a VM restore and observed BSODs, requiring infrastructure on‑call follow‑up)

Step 6.1 (documented practice): Collect the exact change artifacts and timestamps used in prior incident RCAs: WAF/template change events and audit, firewall rule change logs, Azure AD Connect / provisioning script change records, SMTP relay rule change events, and hypervisor/VM restore job entries. The indexed incidents used those artifacts to link a weekend maintenance window to observed failures

Step 6.2 (RECOMMENDED): If you do not have a consolidated change export, query your Change Management system and device audit logs for the weekend window(s) that match the first symptom timestamps and attach those CRs to this incident (the records recommend doing this when weekend/out‑of‑hours changes correlate with outages)

Step 7.1 (documented practice): Preserve logs and configuration snapshots used for remediation (the indexed tickets show attachments, blueprint retrieval and log preservation were used as evidence); attach them to the incident ticket for later postmortem

The knowledge base contains multiple prior incidents that map to each symptom and documents the successful remediation actions above (selective SMTP AUTH/Conditional Access fixes for Outbox; Teams federation/SBC fixes for presence/calls; SMTP relay size adjustments or MFP profile tweaks for large scans; spooler/share and network/VLAN fixes for vanished print jobs). Those prior tickets are the authoritative precedent for each action

However, the records do not contain a single, consolidated weekend change log or a single change‑ID that lists *all* changes across perimeter WAF, firewall, AD Connect, mail relay and print‑server restore that were applied in your environment in one maintenance window. The indexed corpus shows multiple discrete changes across devices/sites but no single CMDB export that I can quote to confirm causation

Azure AD Conditional Access / Security Defaults change record and timestamp; Exchange/Smtp server logs showing 535/“authentication unsuccessful”; message trace showing desktop client submission attempts
Teams Admin Center: federation/presence policy change audit; SBC logs and SIP traces for failed calls; tenant federation changes and any provisioning script change records
SMTP relay rejection logs (look for “MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT”), MFP device scan logs showing SMTP dialogue and message size, and mail relay config change history

Printserver System/Application/PrintService event logs showing spooler state and share publish events; network/VLAN change tickets + switch port mappings; hypervisor restore logs if a VM restore was performed
For each item above the indexed records show those artifacts were used to diagnose and resolve the matching prior incidents

Recommendation (documented pattern): classify this as second_level_support and escalate now to Identity (Entra/AD), Mail/Exchange, Network & Security (firewall/WAF/SBC), and Infrastructure/Print owners. The indexed knowledge shows identical multi‑domain incidents were handled by those specialist teams, not by front‑line only

- To Identity/Entra team (for SMTP/Outbox + Teams presence): “Please export Conditional Access / Security Defaults changes and Azure AD sign‑in logs for affected users (time window: <first‑symptom‑time ± 4h>). Also export group‑membership snapshots and Azure AD Connect sync run history for that window. Ticket refs: <this_ticket>. Prior incidents show these artifacts are decisive.

- To Mail/Relay/MFP owner (for large scans): “Please provide SMTP relay logs and MFP SMTP dialog logs for the time of the failed 20–30 page scans and the relay config (max‑message‑size) history. Look for ‘MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS’ entries. Prior tickets used the same log lines to confirm cause.

- To Network/WAF/SBC owner: “Provide perimeter WAF/template change audit and load‑balancer access logs for the portal FQDN (time window: <anchor>). Also provide firewall rule change history for Zurich and VLAN/routing changes for Basel. Prior incidents show template reloads and firewall rule changes caused similar multi‑service faults.

I cannot, from the indexed knowledge alone, prove a single causal change that produced all four symptoms — the knowledge base shows the same classes of changes (tenant policy, provisioning scripts, firewall/WAF template reloads, relay limits, VM restores) have produced identical symptoms in separate incidents, but it does not contain a single consolidated change log tying them together for your environment. You must collect and correlate the device‑level change/audit logs and the Change Management records named above to confirm causation
Desktop mail / SMTP AUTH root cause and remediation example
Teams federation / presence and Direct‑Routing SBC examples
Scan‑to‑Mail / SMTP relay size rejection and remediation
SAP/printserver VM restore, spooler/share remediation, BSOD observation
Portal/WAF template reload (Default‑Strict → Portal‑App‑Template / X‑Forwarded‑Proto) and blueprint use
